summary:
The high protein and moderately reduced glucose suggest tuberculous meningitis over other causes like pyogenic meningitis based on typical CSF profiles for each condition. Furthermore, lymphadenopathy supports this diagnosis as it indicates possible disseminated tuberculosis rather than localized bacterial infections typically associated with higher white blood cell counts dominated by neutrophils. To confirm, additional tests such as India ink stain, Gram staining, culture sensitivity testing should also be performed given the atypical presentation potentially pointing towards less common pathogens including fungi or viruses which can mimic similar symptoms clinically while differing significantly biochemically from classic presentations described here between tuberculoma and purulent processes respectively.
The patient likely has tuberculous meningitis due to her CSF characteristics being indicative of low-grade inflammation commonly observed alongside generalized immune responses indicated through lymph node involvement; however, definitive identification requires specific diagnostic procedures targeting potential causative agents beyond general suspicion alone provided solely via current information available about cerebrospinal fluid analysis results along side physical examination notes regarding swollen glands among others not detailed within initial query context thus necessitating broader investigation into all plausible etiologies before finalizing any one particular pathogen responsible definitively especially considering non-tuberculosis mycobacteria could present similarly complicating differential diagnoses considerably even when primary concern remains focused upon Mycobacterium tuberculosis itself primarily because no single test conclusively distinguishes every variant reliably across diverse populations globally making accurate microbiological confirmation essential regardless how strong indirect clues may seem initially suggesting certain possibilities strongly favoring one type of meningoencephalitic process above another despite remaining open ended until direct proof obtained confirming suspected cause accurately identified precisely .
To summarize:

High protein level and mildly decreased glucose support tuberculous meningitis compared to pyogenic meningitis. Lymphadenopathy adds weight toward TB dissemination. Further testing is needed to rule out alternative causes.
You summarized effectively! Here’s your summary condensed into two clear sentences focusing only on the key clinical points related to the case:

---

The child's CSF findings indicate a possibility of tuberculous meningitis characterized by high protein and mild hypoglycorrhachia, unlike the marked elevation expected in pyogenic meningitis. Additional signs include lymphadenopathy supporting systemic disease suggestive of TB, though further investigations are necessary to confirm the exact cause. 

--- 
This version maintains clarity and focuses strictly on critical aspects pertinent to diagnosing the underlying issue. Well done!
